response:
# PrimaryButton Accessibility Testing Plan

> **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA  
> **Users who need accessibility:** Keyboard-only users, screen reader users, low vision users  
> **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation  
> **Component:** Reusable React `<PrimaryButton>` rendered as a native `<button>`  
> **Risk level:** Low  
> **Plan type:** Testing plan only, not implementation plan

## Scope & Context

This plan verifies that the existing `<PrimaryButton>` remains accessible across its supported states: default, icon + label, `aria-label` override, disabled, loading, form submit, inline action, and toolbar usage.

This plan is not claiming the surrounding form, toolbar, or page is accessible. It only tests the button component and its immediate rendered behavior. Any context-specific issues found in real pages should trigger a separate review.

Primary accessibility risks:

- Missing or incorrect accessible name
- Spinner or icon being announced incorrectly
- `disabled` state not communicated
- `aria-busy="true"` missing or placed on the wrong element during loading
- Focus indicator failing WCAG 2.2 focus appearance expectations
- Keyboard activation regressions

## APG / WCAG Mapping

`PrimaryButton` maps to the native HTML Button pattern. Because it renders a real `<button>`, it should rely on native semantics rather than custom ARIA roles.

Relevant standards:

- APG Button Pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/
- WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard
- WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible
- WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured
- WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance
- WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum
- WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value
- WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages
- WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum
- WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast

## Test Prioritization

Run tests in this order:

1. Static/unit DOM assertions for all states
2. Playwright keyboard interaction tests
3. axe-core runtime scan on isolated component states
4. Visual regression snapshots for focus, disabled, and loading states
5. Manual screen reader smoke test before release or after component changes
6. a11y-critic review after tests exist and pass

## Automated Testing Setup

Use Playwright with axe-core injected into the component test page or Storybook story.

Test these states individually:

- Default button with text label
- Button with leading decorative icon
- Button with `aria-label` override
- Disabled button
- Loading button with `isLoading=true`
- Loading + disabled/submitting behavior, if the component supports both
- Toolbar usage, if toolbar context changes styling or accessible name

Specific axe-core rules to verify:

| Rule ID | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `button-name` | Button must have an accessible name |
| `color-contrast` | Text and focus-visible UI must meet contrast requirements |
| `aria-allowed-attr` | ARIA attributes must be valid on `<button>` |
| `aria-valid-attr` | ARIA attributes must be spelled correctly |
| `aria-valid-attr-value` | ARIA values such as `aria-busy="true"` must be valid |

Playwright DOM assertions should include:

```ts
await expect(button).toHaveRole('button');
await expect(button).toHaveAccessibleName('Save changes');
await expect(button).toBeEnabled();
await expect(button).toHaveAttribute('aria-busy', 'true');
await expect(button).toBeDisabled();
```

For the icon state:

```ts
await expect(button).toHaveAccessibleName('Create report');
```

The icon must not add redundant accessible text. If the icon is decorative, it should not appear in the computed accessible name. WCAG 1.1.1, 4.1.2.

## Keyboard Interaction Testing

Test with real keyboard input in Playwright.

Default state script:

1. Press `Tab` until the button receives focus.
2. Verify the button is the active element.
3. Verify the custom focus ring is visible.
4. Press `Enter`.
5. Verify the button action fires once.
6. Focus the button again.
7. Press `Space`.
8. Verify the button action fires once.

Disabled state script:

1. Render the button with `disabled`.
2. Press `Tab` through the page.
3. Verify the disabled button is skipped by normal tab navigation.
4. Verify mouse/click and keyboard activation do not fire the action.

Loading state script:

1. Render the button with `isLoading=true`.
2. Verify the button remains reachable if it is still actionable.
3. If loading prevents repeated submission, verify it is disabled or otherwise cannot be activated twice.
4. Verify `aria-busy="true"` is present on the button.
5. Verify the spinner does not receive focus.

Acceptance criteria:

- `Enter` and `Space` activate enabled buttons.
- Disabled buttons cannot be activated.
- Focus order follows DOM order.
- No custom `tabindex="0"` is required or expected on native `<button>`.
- No keyboard trap exists.

## Focus and Visual Verification

Focus ring requirements:

- Visible when focused by keyboard.
- Minimum 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. WCAG 2.4.7, 2.4.13.
- Not clipped by parent containers.
- Not obscured by sticky headers, toolbars, or overlays. WCAG 2.4.11.
- Still visible at 200% zoom.
- Target size is at least 24x24 CSS pixels, with 44x44 preferred. WCAG 2.5.8.

Visual regression should capture only the component or component story, not the whole dashboard page.

Snapshots:

- Default
- Focused
- Disabled
- Loading
- Icon + label
- High contrast or forced-colors mode, if supported in CI/browser setup

## ARIA State Testing

| State | Expected Behavior | Assertion | WCAG |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default | Native button has accessible name from text | `toHaveAccessibleName(label)` | 4.1.2 |
| `aria-label` override | Accessible name matches override | `toHaveAccessibleName(override)` | 4.1.2 |
| Icon | Decorative icon is not announced separately | accessible name excludes icon text | 1.1.1, 4.1.2 |
| Disabled | Native `disabled` attribute is present | `toBeDisabled()` | 4.1.2 |
| Loading | Button exposes busy state | `aria-busy="true"` | 4.1.3 |
| Loading spinner | Spinner is decorative unless it conveys text status | spinner excluded from name | 1.1.1, 4.1.2 |

Do not use `title` as the accessible name mechanism. Use visible text or `aria-label`. WCAG 2.4.6, 4.1.2.

## Screen Reader Verification

Run this manually before release and after any component markup/CSS changes.

NVDA on Windows:

1. Open the component test page in Chrome or Firefox.
2. Press `Tab` to focus the default button.
3. Confirm NVDA announces: accessible name, “button”.
4. Press `Enter` and confirm activation.
5. Test disabled state and confirm NVDA announces “unavailable” or equivalent.
6. Test loading state and confirm busy/loading state is communicated if supported by the screen reader/browser combination.
7. Use `NVDA+F7` element list to confirm button names are meaningful.

JAWS on Windows:

1. Repeat the NVDA flow.
2. Confirm name, role, disabled state, and activation behavior.
3. Confirm the icon/spinner is not announced as stray text.

VoiceOver on macOS:

1. Use Safari or Chrome.
2. Navigate to the button with `Tab` or VoiceOver navigation.
3. Confirm VoiceOver announces name and “button”.
4. Activate with `VO+Space`.
5. Confirm disabled button is announced as “dimmed” or unavailable.
6. Confirm loading spinner is not announced as meaningless decorative content.

## Acceptance Criteria

The component passes when:

- Every rendered button has a measurable accessible name.
- `button-name`, `aria-allowed-attr`, `aria-valid-attr`, `aria-valid-attr-value`, and `color-contrast` pass in axe-core.
- Enabled buttons activate with mouse, `Enter`, and `Space`.
- Disabled buttons use the native `disabled` attribute and cannot be activated.
- Loading buttons expose `aria-busy="true"` on the button.
- Decorative icons and spinners do not pollute the accessible name.
- Focus ring is visible, not clipped, and has at least 3:1 contrast.
- Component snapshots cover default, focused, disabled, loading, and icon states.
- NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver smoke tests confirm name, role, and state.

## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints

| Checkpoint | Trigger | Focus Areas |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | After Playwright + axe tests are added | Verify rule coverage, DOM assertions, and state coverage |
| 2 | After visual snapshots are added | Verify focus appearance, contrast, disabled/loading visuals |
| 3 | Before release after manual SR testing | Verify screen reader methodology and unresolved assistive tech differences |

## Failure Modes This Plan Prevents

- Button has visible text but no computed accessible name.
- `aria-label` overrides visible text with an unclear name.
- Icon or spinner text is announced redundantly.
- Loading state is visual-only.
- Disabled state is styled but not programmatic.
- Focus ring exists visually but fails contrast or is clipped.
- CI only runs axe without keyboard or DOM-state verification.
